stem: The line \( y = [[m]]x + ([[c]]) \) and the parabola \( y = x^{2} \) intersect at two points. Find the area of the region enclosed between them.
solution: The curves meet where \( x^{2} = [[m]]x + ([[c]]) \), i.e. at \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \); between these the line lies above the parabola, so the area is \( \int_{[[r1]]}^{[[r2]]}\left(([[m]]x+([[c]])) - x^{2}\right)dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct area, as if the leading antiderivative factor were \( \tfrac13 \) instead of \( \tfrac16 \).
This halves the correct area.
This is the square of the horizontal width between the intersection points, not the enclosed area.
This integrates bottom minus top instead of top minus bottom, giving the negative of the area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: The curve \( y = (x-[[a]])([[b]]-x) \) touches the \( x \)-axis at \( x=[[a]] \) and \( x=[[b]] \) and lies above it in between. Find the area of the region enclosed between the curve and the \( x \)-axis.
solution: This is a downward-opening arch between its two roots; expanding and integrating, \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}(x-[[a]])([[b]]-x)\,dx = \dfrac{([[b]]-[[a]])^{3}}{6} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \tfrac13 \) instead of the correct \( \tfrac16 \) coefficient in the closed-form area formula for this arch.
This is the square of the base width \( [[b]]-[[a]] \), not an area under a curved arch.
This doubles the correct area.
This integrates the curve with the wrong sign, giving the negative of the enclosed area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: Find the area of the region enclosed between the sideways parabola \( x = y^{2} + ([[p]]) \) and the vertical line \( x = [[c]] \).
solution: The curves meet where \( y^{2}+([[p]])=[[c]] \), i.e. \( y=\pm [[m]] \). Integrating horizontal strips of width \( [[c]] - (y^{2}+([[p]])) \) (the line minus the parabola, as functions of \( y \)) gives \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]}\left([[c]] - (y^{2}+([[p]]))\right)dy = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct area.
This halves the correct area.
This treats the region as a \( 2\cdot[[m]] \) by \( 2\cdot[[m]] \) by \( 2\cdot[[m]] \) box instead of integrating the strip widths.
This integrates the strip width with the wrong sign, giving the negative of the area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: The curve \( y = (x-[[a]])(x-[[b]]) \) lies BELOW the \( x \)-axis on \( ([[a]],[[b]]) \) and ABOVE it elsewhere. Find the TOTAL area of the region(s) enclosed between the curve and the \( x \)-axis on the interval \( [[lo]] \le x \le [[hi]] \).
solution: Since the curve changes sign at \( x=[[a]] \) and \( x=[[b]] \), the TOTAL enclosed area needs \( \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]}\left|(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])\right|dx = [[answer]] \), not the plain (signed) integral, which would let the regions cancel.
distractor_rationales: This is the plain SIGNED integral \( \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]}(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])\,dx \), which lets the positive and negative regions partially cancel.
This only accounts for the dip between the roots \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \), ignoring the regions above the axis outside them.
This halves the correct total area.
This doubles the correct total area.
This is off by one from the correct total area.
